steps.title: Spot the list of three factors with internal commas
steps.content: The sentence introduces three main factors with “—namely,”. The second factor contains an internal comma (“with higher-velocity meteoroids releasing more energy”), and the third factor also contains an internal comma (“such as changes in sky conditions”). When list items have commas inside them, they require a stronger separator between items.

steps.title: Apply the semicolon rule for complex lists
steps.content:
When items in a list contain commas, the SAT uses semicolons to separate the major items, creating the pattern Item 1; Item 2; and Item 3. Here, that means: “the size and composition of the meteoroids; their velocity upon entering Earth's mesosphere, with higher-velocity meteoroids releasing more energy; and atmospheric fluctuations, such as changes in sky conditions.” Choice A achieves this structure by using a semicolon. Choices C and D use a comma and em dash (both too weak to separate complex items), and choice B uses a period that inappropriately ends the sentence mid-list.

That matches choice A — A is correct.
